items.prompt_md: In **pre-order** traversal, when does a node do its own work relative to its two recursive calls?
options: Before either recursive call
Between the left and right recursive calls
After both recursive calls have returned
Only on leaf nodes
items.answer: Before either recursive call
items.explanation_md: Pre-order means 'the node first, then its children' — you handle the current node's own work, then recurse left, then recurse right.

items.prompt_md: In **in-order** traversal, when does a node do its own work relative to its two recursive calls?
options: Before either recursive call
Between the left and right recursive calls
After both recursive calls have returned
In-order traversal never visits the node itself, only its children
items.answer: Between the left and right recursive calls
items.explanation_md: In-order visits left, then the node itself, then right — the node's own work is sandwiched between the two recursive calls.

items.prompt_md: In **post-order** traversal, when does a node do its own work relative to its two recursive calls?
options: Before either recursive call
Between the left and right recursive calls
After both recursive calls have returned
Post-order skips the node's own work entirely
items.answer: After both recursive calls have returned
items.explanation_md: Post-order means 'children first, then the node' — both recursive calls finish before the current node touches its own result.

items.prompt_md: What should a recursive DFS function do first, before touching `root.left` or `root.right`?
options: Check whether `root` is `None` and handle that base case immediately
Swap `root.left` and `root.right`
Push `root` onto an explicit stack
Compute `1 + max(left, right)`
items.answer: Check whether `root` is `None` and handle that base case immediately
items.explanation_md: Every recursive tree function needs a base case for the empty subtree; skipping it means the very next line dereferences `None` and crashes.

items.prompt_md: For inverting a binary tree, why does **post-order** (recurse first, then swap) work correctly?
options: Because the swap at a node doesn't depend on anything outside that node's own two children
Because post-order is the only order Python allows for recursive functions
Because post-order visits the root twice
Because it avoids using recursion at all
items.answer: Because the swap at a node doesn't depend on anything outside that node's own two children
items.explanation_md: The swap is a purely local operation, so it doesn't matter whether it happens before or after the children are fixed — both orders reach every node exactly once.

items.prompt_md: For inverting a binary tree, why does **pre-order** (swap first, then recurse into the now-swapped children) also work correctly?
options: Because swapping a node's children first and then recursing into them still visits and fixes every node exactly once
Because pre-order is faster than post-order for this problem
Because pre-order avoids the base case entirely
Because swapping twice cancels out any ordering issues
items.answer: Because swapping a node's children first and then recursing into them still visits and fixes every node exactly once
items.explanation_md: Whether you swap before or after recursing, each node's children get swapped exactly once — the order only matters when one node's work depends on a sibling or ancestor's result, which isn't the case here.

items.prompt_md: What is the main reason to convert a recursive DFS into an **iterative DFS with an explicit stack**?
options: To avoid hitting the language's recursion-depth limit or exhausting the call stack on a very deep (e.g. skewed) tree
To reduce the traversal's time complexity below O(n)
Because explicit stacks visit fewer nodes than recursion
Because recursion cannot process leaf nodes correctly
items.answer: To avoid hitting the language's recursion-depth limit or exhausting the call stack on a very deep (e.g. skewed) tree
items.explanation_md: A recursive call uses one stack frame per level of depth. On a pathologically deep or skewed tree, that can overflow the call stack; an explicit stack on the heap sidesteps the language's recursion limit.

items.prompt_md: When simulating **pre-order** with your own explicit stack, in what order should you push a node's children so that `left` is processed before `right`?
options: Push `right` first, then `left` — so `left` ends up on top and pops first
Push `left` first, then `right` — so `left` pops first
Order doesn't matter for pre-order
Push both at the same time using a single call
items.answer: Push `right` first, then `left` — so `left` ends up on top and pops first
items.explanation_md: A stack is LIFO, so whichever child is pushed last is processed first. To visit `left` before `right`, push `right` onto the stack before `left`.

items.prompt_md: A recursive DFS function forgets to check for `root is None` and instead only guards `root.left is None` and `root.right is None` separately before recursing. What bug does this risk?
options: Calling the function on a `None` child still runs the function body and crashes trying to read `root.left`/`root.right` off `None`
The traversal silently skips the root node
The traversal visits every node twice
There is no bug — this is equivalent to a `root is None` check
items.answer: Calling the function on a `None` child still runs the function body and crashes trying to read `root.left`/`root.right` off `None`
items.explanation_md: Guarding the children before the recursive call doesn't stop the function itself from being entered with `root = None` on the very next level down; the base case must live at the top of the function, not at the call site.

items.prompt_md: You need to record node values in **sorted order** for a binary search tree. Which traversal order gives you that directly?
options: In-order (left, node, right)
Pre-order (node, left, right)
Post-order (left, right, node)
Level-order (breadth-first)
items.answer: In-order (left, node, right)
items.explanation_md: In a BST, in-order traversal visits smaller values before larger ones because it always fully drains the left subtree before the node, and the node before the right subtree.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of a recursive DFS that visits every node of an n-node binary tree once, doing O(1) work per node?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(h), where h is the tree's height
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Each of the n nodes is visited exactly once with constant work per node, so the total is O(n) regardless of the tree's shape.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of recursive DFS, in terms of the tree's height h, and how does it behave in the worst case?
options: O(h) for the call stack, which degrades to O(n) for a completely skewed tree
O(1), since recursion doesn't use any extra memory
O(n) always, regardless of the tree's shape
O(log n) always, since trees are assumed balanced
items.answer: O(h) for the call stack, which degrades to O(n) for a completely skewed tree
items.explanation_md: The call stack holds at most one frame per level of depth, so it's O(h) — O(log n) for a balanced tree, but O(n) if the tree degenerates into a single chain.

items.prompt_md: You need to invert a binary tree — recursively swap every node's left and right children. Which approach fits?
options: DFS recursion, swapping a node's children either before or after recursing into them
BFS with a queue, tracking one level's node count at a time
A hash map keyed by node value to detect swapped pairs
Two pointers converging from the tree's leftmost and rightmost leaves
items.answer: DFS recursion, swapping a node's children either before or after recursing into them
items.explanation_md: Each node's swap only depends on its own two children, so pre/post-order DFS recursion — the tree's own recursive definition — is the direct fit.

items.prompt_md: You need to multiply every node's value in a binary tree by 2, in place, visiting each node exactly once, with no ordering constraint between nodes. Which approach fits?
options: DFS recursion doing the multiply at each node, before or after recursing
Bottom-up recursion that combines each node's value with its children's values into one returned number
BFS with a queue, processing one full level before the next
A sliding window scanned over the node values
items.answer: DFS recursion doing the multiply at each node, before or after recursing
items.explanation_md: This is a per-node transform with no combining of children's results, so plain pre/post-order DFS recursion fits — not an aggregation pattern and not a level-by-level one.

items.prompt_md: The binary tree you must traverse may be up to 100,000 nodes deep in a single skewed chain, and your language's recursion limit is far smaller than that. You still need to visit every node exactly once. Which approach fits?
options: Iterative DFS using your own explicit stack instead of the call stack
Plain recursive DFS, since recursion depth never matters
BFS with a queue, since it uses no stack at all
Bottom-up aggregate recursion, since it returns values instead of recursing
items.answer: Iterative DFS using your own explicit stack instead of the call stack
items.explanation_md: Recursion would need one stack frame per level of depth and could overflow on a chain this deep; simulating the same DFS with an explicit, heap-allocated stack sidesteps the recursion-depth limit.

items.prompt_md: You must print a binary search tree's values from smallest to largest, using its own structure rather than sorting an array afterward. Which approach fits?
options: In-order DFS recursion (left, node, right)
Pre-order DFS recursion (node, left, right)
BFS with a queue, one level at a time
A hash map keyed by node value
items.answer: In-order DFS recursion (left, node, right)
items.explanation_md: In-order traversal of a BST visits nodes in ascending value order for free, because it always finishes the smaller left subtree before the node and the node before the larger right subtree.

items.prompt_md: Given an unsorted integer array, you need to find two numbers that add up to a target value, in a single pass. Which approach fits?
options: A hash map that stores each seen value and checks for its complement as you scan
DFS recursion over the array treated as a tree
An explicit stack that pushes and pops array elements
Bottom-up aggregate recursion that returns a combined sum
items.answer: A hash map that stores each seen value and checks for its complement as you scan
items.explanation_md: There's no tree here — a flat array with a target-sum lookup is the hash map pattern's signature, not a DFS traversal.

items.prompt_md: You need to reverse a singly linked list in place, in one pass, using only a few pointer variables. Which approach fits?
options: Walk the list, reversing each node's `next` pointer to point at the previous node as you go
DFS recursion, doing the node's own work before or after recursing into its children
BFS with a queue, snapshotting how many nodes are on the current level
A hash map keyed by node value
items.answer: Walk the list, reversing each node's `next` pointer to point at the previous node as you go
items.explanation_md: A singly linked list has no children to recurse into — it's a straight chain, so pointer-reversal is the linked-list pattern, not a tree traversal.

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether a string of `(`, `)`, `{`, `}`, `[`, `]` characters is balanced. Which approach fits?
options: Push opening brackets onto a stack and pop to match each closing bracket
DFS recursion over the characters treated as tree nodes
BFS with a queue, processing characters level by level
Bottom-up aggregate recursion returning a combined boolean
items.answer: Push opening brackets onto a stack and pop to match each closing bracket
items.explanation_md: Matching nested pairs in the most-recent-first order is the textbook stack pattern — there's no tree structure to traverse here.

items.prompt_md: You need to collect every node's value into a flat list, visiting the root, then its whole left subtree, then its whole right subtree. Which approach fits?
options: Pre-order DFS recursion, appending the node's value before recursing into its children
BFS with a queue, appending one level at a time
Two pointers converging from the tree's two extreme leaves
A hash map keyed by depth
items.answer: Pre-order DFS recursion, appending the node's value before recursing into its children
items.explanation_md: 'Root, then whole left subtree, then whole right subtree' is pre-order by definition — recurse depth-first, recording each node's value before its children.

items.prompt_md: You must find the closest pair of values to each other within a sorted array. Which approach fits?
options: Two pointers scanning inward from both ends of the sorted array
DFS recursion, doing work before or after recursing into children
BFS with a queue, tracking one level's node count
An explicit stack simulating recursive calls
items.answer: Two pointers scanning inward from both ends of the sorted array
items.explanation_md: A sorted array with a pairwise-closeness question is the two-pointers signature — there's no tree to descend into here.

items.prompt_md: You need to make a deep copy of a binary tree — build a brand-new set of nodes that mirrors the original's structure and values exactly. Which approach fits?
options: DFS recursion, creating a new node at each step and recursing into the original's children
BFS with a queue, only tracking the last node seen on each level
A hash map keyed by node value to detect duplicates
Two pointers converging from the tree's leftmost and rightmost leaves
items.answer: DFS recursion, creating a new node at each step and recursing into the original's children
items.explanation_md: Building a new node whose children are the (recursively copied) new versions of the original's children is a direct pre-order or post-order DFS — the recursive structure mirrors the tree's own definition.

items.prompt_md: You need to determine whether a path from the root to some leaf sums to an exact target value, stopping as soon as you know the answer. Which approach fits?
options: DFS recursion, carrying the running sum down and checking it at each leaf
BFS with a queue, returning the first fully-built level
A hash map keyed by node depth
Two pointers starting at the root and the deepest leaf
items.answer: DFS recursion, carrying the running sum down and checking it at each leaf
items.explanation_md: Root-to-leaf path questions are naturally depth-first — you extend the running sum as you recurse and check it once you reach a leaf (a base case).

items.prompt_md: You need to count how many times each value appears in a flat integer array. Which approach fits?
options: A hash map that tallies a running count keyed by value as you scan the array once
DFS recursion over the array treated as tree nodes
BFS with a queue, one array segment 'level' at a time
Bottom-up aggregate recursion returning a combined count
items.answer: A hash map that tallies a running count keyed by value as you scan the array once
items.explanation_md: There's no tree here — tallying frequencies over a flat array in one pass is the hash map pattern's signature, not a tree traversal.
